reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher professional autonomy, workload sustainability, and staffing feasibility. The documented value base is the regulatory and collective-bargaining framework governing teacher time allocation, assessment design responsibility, and administrative burden (Nkt. 2011; Hungarian Teachers' Union position statements on assessment and reporting burden).
reactions.interest: Teachers and school leaders responsible for admission assessment, intake monitoring, and outreach execution; institutional capacity to absorb new administrative and curriculum design tasks without workload degradation.
reactions.public_good_frame: Sustainable reform requires that admission-rule change does not cascade into unmanageable assessment design, proxy-gaming audits, and outreach tracking that shifts workload from instruction to compliance. Teacher involvement in valid assessment design (not mere implementation of untested instruments) is a public good: it ensures assessment integrity and reduces coaching vulnerability more reliably than top-down instrument replacement.
reactions.argument: S1 imposes three workload and autonomy risks: (1) The scenario itself flags that a 'less-coachable assessment' is unverified; if Oktatási Hivatal pilots it at system scale without prior small-scale co-design with teachers, the instrument is likely to be psychometrically weak and teachers will bear the reputational and instructional cost of a failed tool. (2) A SES-proxy 'gameable' by families creates recurring auditing burden on school leaders, who will spend time investigating proxy manipulation rather than instructional leadership. (3) Outreach and application-uptake tracking are mandated to civil society and maintainers but require schools to administer and report uptake data; if this is unfunded or added to existing reporting, it compounds administrative burden. Condition: I would support S1 only if (a) assessment redesign is genuinely co-designed with secondary teachers in a 2-year pilot before system roll-out, with psychometric validation that includes coaching-sensitivity testing supervised by teachers; (b) any SES proxy is audited once at baseline, then monitored passively via annual intake reports (not active gaming audits added to school compliance); and (c) outreach and uptake tracking is funded as a separate staffing line (not absorbed into general-school administrative time).
reactions.condition_to_change: Co-design of assessment with teachers; audited SES proxy with passive monitoring only; dedicated outreach staffing funded separately from school budgets. Reverse my support if the assessment is imposed top-down without pilot co-design, if auditing for proxy-gaming is added to school leader compliance, or if outreach tracking becomes unfunded school responsibility.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher job security, workload during transition, and feasibility of staffing general-school upper grades with adequate teaching capacity. The documented value base is the Nkt. statutory notice and transitional-guarantee framework; teacher-shortage findings; and the collective-bargaining principle that structural change affecting employment must include retraining and job-protection guarantees.
reactions.interest: Teachers in selective-track schools facing potential closure or merger; teachers in general-school upper grades who will absorb retained cohorts; teacher unions negotiating employment protections during the decade-long phase-down.
reactions.public_good_frame: A phased cap on selective seats can reduce early sorting inequity only if it does not degrade instruction in general-school upper grades by overcrowding classrooms or forcing under-qualified staff into newly enlarged cohorts. Honoring statutory notice and grandfathering is not merely a legal obligation; it is a workload and morale condition: teachers in closed selective tracks need retraining guarantees, and general-school teachers need class-size and support commitments, not just intake expansion.
reactions.argument: S2's primary risk from the teacher-workload and staffing perspective is the parallel-regime cost: 'administration while grandfathered cohorts run under old rules alongside capped intake' is coded as 'medium, but persisting across most of the decade'. For school leaders and teachers, this is actually high burden: mixed cohorts of grandfathered and capped students create dual curriculum, dual assessment, and unequal resource allocation within the same school for 8-10 years. This is unsustainable without either (a) splitting cohorts into separate facilities (infrastructure cost not in the costing) or (b) accepting that grandfathered cohorts receive lower instructional quality as school attention drifts to capped-intake planning. Additionally, general-school upper grades must absorb the retained cohort without corresponding class-size reduction or teacher hiring; if the teacher shortage persists (and the scenario assumes it does), this is a direct workload increase per teacher with no relief. Finally, the non-state offset risk ('strong' in the scenario) means that if church and foundation schools absorb displaced selective demand, the policy fails on its own equity terms, and teachers in public general schools have borne the burden of transition with no outcome gain. Condition: I would support S2 only if (a) statutory notice is genuinely 3-5 years (not 1-2 as the implementation suggests), allowing selective-track teachers time for retraining and placement without layoffs; (b) general-school upper grades are guaranteed class-size caps (e.g., max 28 pupils per class) and corresponding teacher hiring funded by the cap proceeds; (c) the Ministry legislates intake limits for non-state maintainers simultaneously with the cap, backed by enforcement and annual public reporting; and (d) the cohort of selective-track teachers facing closure is offered retraining with salary protection, not wage reduction.
reactions.condition_to_change: Statutory notice extended to 3-5 years; class-size caps and funded hiring for general-school upper grades; simultaneous and enforceable non-state intake limits; retraining and salary protection for selective-track teachers. Reverse my support if notice is <3 years, if general-school class sizes are not capped with funding, if non-state maintainers are not legislatively limited, or if selective-track teachers face wage cuts or forced retraining without job protection.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher supply stability, professional autonomy in curriculum differentiation, and workload sustainability under structural reform. The documented value base is the strong teacher-shortage finding; the comparative education evidence (Finland, Poland) that comprehensive reform requires prior and parallel teacher-quality investment; and the Hungarian teachers' unions' position that structural change without teacher-supply stabilisation and accountability decoupling creates unsustainable workload and professional deskilling (Round 6 stakeholder analysis).
reactions.interest: Teachers in both state and non-state secondary schools; teacher supply and retention; school leaders responsible for hiring and managing mixed-ability upper-secondary comprehensive cohorts; teacher unions negotiating employment and professional conditions.
reactions.public_good_frame: Abolishing early selection is educationally justified only if it does not degrade instruction by forcing teachers to manage unmanageable mixed-ability classrooms without support or to teach with inadequate differentiation tools. Teacher supply is the rate-limiting step: if Hungary cannot stabilise and expand the teaching workforce, structural change creates worse inequity, not better, because teachers will leave, schools will hire under-qualified staff, and disadvantaged schools will bear the brunt. Professional autonomy in curriculum differentiation is a public good: teachers need the time, training, and diagnostic tools to respond to mixed-ability students; if structural change is imposed without these, instruction becomes surface-level and inequity increases.
reactions.argument: S3 is the scenario with the highest expected equity gain but is opposed because the teacher-supply constraint is documented as 'strong' and is a binding, non-negotiable precondition that the scenario does not resolve. The scenario itself states that 'the teacher-shortage constraint' is a 'strong binding constraint' and that assumptions include 'teacher supply can be stabilised enough to staff comprehensive upper grades without degrading quality' — which it cannot, based on the input findings. Implementing S3 without first solving teacher supply is equivalent to imposing structural change on a system without the human-capital base to deliver it; the result will be mixed-ability classes with under-qualified or burnt-out teachers, which will harm equity more than current early selection because it will concentrate teacher-shortage effects on comprehensive upper grades. Additionally, the scenario assumes 'teacher unions withdraw conditional support if accountability decoupling is not delivered' — meaning unions are willing to support S3 only under conditions (retraining, accountability shield, time for differentiation) that are not funded or scheduled in the implementation roadmap. The political-durability assumption (surviving two electoral cycles) is also not credible for teachers: if comprehensive reform is reversed within 8 years, as Poland's was, teachers who requalify and restructure their practice will face re-reversal, undermining morale and retention further. I have no position on whether comprehensive reform is educationally justified in abstract; I oppose S3 specifically because its implementation timeline and preconditions are mismatched to the teacher-supply reality documented in the inputs.
reactions.condition_to_change: I would reverse to 'conditional support' only if: (a) teacher supply is demonstrably stabilised (i.e., the teacher-shortage finding is superseded by evidence of recruitment and retention improvements, tracking vacancies and salary across regions for 2+ years) before S3 implementation begins; (b) accountability decoupling is legislated and funded in a way that teachers' unions publicly endorse, with specific ring-fenced time for professional development in differentiated instruction and diagnostic assessment; (c) a pilot phase in a single region with matched comparison groups runs for 5+ years with independent outcome evaluation and teacher-workload tracking before national scale-up is decided; and (d) cross-party or constitutional embedding is in place to prevent reversal within two electoral cycles. Absent all four, S3 imposes on teachers the burden of structural change without the conditions to deliver it, and I oppose it on that ground alone.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher workload, morale, and the viability of instruction in disadvantaged schools under chronic underfunding. The documented value base is the teacher-shortage finding ('strong'); the budget-vulnerability political risk that 'ring-fenced equity funding is the first cut in fiscal consolidation'; and the comparative evidence (Portugal via TEIP, implied; Finnish reform evidence) that compensation packages require sustained investment in teacher training, special education, and support staffing that is politically vulnerable in Hungarian budgetary cycles.
reactions.interest: Teachers in disadvantaged general schools; school leaders in under-resourced settings who must implement compensation without stable funding or teacher supply; teacher unions negotiating wage and working-condition floor agreements in schools subject to resource volatility.
reactions.public_good_frame: A public good is not served by asking teachers in disadvantaged schools to deliver rigorous curriculum and targeted support without stable funding, adequate staffing, or reasonable class sizes. S4 creates a two-tier teaching workforce: affluent selective schools retain stable staffing and conditions; disadvantaged general schools are asked to deliver higher value-added per pupil while facing budget cuts and teacher shortages. This is not sustainable and will degrade instruction precisely where it is most needed.
reactions.argument: S4 is politically seductive because it avoids structural change, but it is operationally indefensible for teachers from the workload and stability perspective. The scenario itself flags that 'ring-fenced equity funding is the first cut in fiscal consolidation, undermining durability' — this is the death knell for the model. In the Hungarian budgetary cycle (as in most countries), targeted education spending is the most politically vulnerable because it lacks the constituency of affluent parents defending selective schools. If targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is cut after 3-5 years (as it has been historically in Hungary), teachers in those schools face the worst outcome: they are expected to maintain curriculum standards and deliver support without the resources promised. This compounds the teacher-shortage constraint already documented: if disadvantaged schools become progressively under-resourced, they will lose teachers to better-funded selective schools or to career exit. Additionally, S4 assumes 'compensation can meaningfully offset, not just mask, the effects of early sorting' — but the scenario itself marks this as unverifiable ex ante and notes 'no country evidence exists at Hungary's selection age'. Teachers in disadvantaged schools will be told their job is to 'offset' sorting effects through better teaching, but they will lack the staffing, training, and diagnostic tools to do so, and will bear professional blame for inequality they did not create and cannot unilaterally solve. This is a morale and retention disaster. I oppose S4 because it asks teachers in disadvantaged schools to deliver an unproven compensation model with unstable funding and no guarantee of teacher supply, while leaving the sorting mechanism intact. This is not a sustainable or ethical position for the teaching profession.
reactions.condition_to_change: I would shift to 'conditional support' only if: (a) targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is constitutionally protected or embedded in a multi-party agreement with at least 10-year durability, backed by enforcement mechanisms that prevent reversal in fiscal consolidation; (b) teacher recruitment and retention in disadvantaged schools is supported by wage supplements (relative to selective schools) and mentoring networks, funded in the same constitutional or multi-party agreement; (c) curriculum standards and diagnostic assessment tools are co-designed with teachers in disadvantaged schools and validated in a 3-year pilot before rollout; and (d) the government commits to tracking and reporting annually on whether compensation is closing the SES-achievement gap or merely masking it, with explicit performance review at year 5 to decide whether S4 continues or a structural reform is adopted. Absent these, S4 is a budget-vulnerable, teacher-hostile model that I oppose on principle.
